The episode opens at Errol Childress's isolated bayou compound. Errol is shown torturing his bound father in a brick structure while speaking in a shifting British accent. He lives in a sprawling, hoarded-out house alongside a simple-minded woman named Betty. A spiral brand scar is visible on Errol's back, matching the mark seen on Reggie Ledoux.

Rust and Marty force the corrupt Sheriff Geraci to watch the VHS tape of Marie Fontenot's ritualistic abuse. They intimidate him into silence about their investigation, with Rust arranging for a sniper associate to monitor Geraci as insurance.

Through old case files and photographs, Rust and Marty discover that a house painter with a scarred face and green paint on his ears matches the "green-eared spaghetti monster" description from a child witness in 1995. They trace the work orders to "Childress and Son Maintenance," identifying Errol Childress's address.

At the compound, Marty enters the house and discovers Errol's father, Billy Childress, restrained to a bed with his lips sewn shut. The interior reveals mummified remains, piles of child-sized clothing, and elaborate stick sculptures throughout a massive brick structure functioning as a ritualistic temple — "Carcosa." Errol flees into the labyrinthine structure.

Rust pursues Errol into Carcosa, a sprawling underground catacomb-like space filled with stick sculptures and occult imagery. He experiences a hallucinatory vision of a spiraling vortex of darkness. Errol ambushes and stabs Rust with a knife and attacks Marty with a hatchet. Despite severe wounds, Rust shoots Errol fatally, and Marty also manages to shoot him.

Papania and Gilbough arrive with backup. Forensic teams link Errol to dozens of missing persons cases, but the state attorney general discredits any connections to Senator Edwin Tuttle's family, leaving the broader conspiracy unexposed.

Both detectives recover in the hospital. Marty's daughters visit him. Rust, emerging from a coma, describes a near-death experience: he felt surrounded by darkness infused with the love of his deceased daughter and father, fundamentally shifting his nihilistic worldview. In the final scene, Marty wheels Rust outside the hospital. Looking at the night sky, Rust observes that once there was only darkness — and the light is winning. For the first time, pessimism gives way to cautious hope.
